meaning: To turn the tables; to reverse the argument against one's opponent.
origin: Spieß (spear or spit): reversing the pointed weapon so it faces the attacker. Military imagery applied to argumentation. Attested in German since the early modern period.

variants.note: Variant with 'umkehren' (to reverse) instead of 'umdrehen'.
He turned the tables — past tense.

meaning: To reject someone; to turn someone down romantically.
origin: Medieval legend: a woman lowering a suitor in a basket (Korb) through a window, then leaving him dangling — giving him the basket (rejection). The image entered German as a standard expression for romantic refusal.
why_it_matters: One of the most common German expressions for rejection. Understanding the basket image makes the phrase memorable.

variants.note: To get the basket — to be rejected (passive perspective).

variants.surface: sie hat ihm einen Korb gegeben
variants.note: She turned him down — past tense.

meaning: To throw in the towel; to give up.
origin: From boxing: a trainer throwing a towel into the ring concedes the match. Borrowed directly from English boxing culture into German, retaining the same image.

variants.note: He threw in the towel — past tense.
Colloquial variant with 'schmeißen' (to chuck).

meaning: To put one's foot in it; to make an embarrassing blunder.
origin: Fettnäpfchen (small grease dish): small pots of grease were placed near doors or under chairs for polishing boots. Stepping into one was a social embarrassment — the word stuck as a metaphor for unintentional offense.
why_it_matters: A quintessentially German idiom with no clean English parallel. The Fettnäpfchen image is opaque and impossible to guess — a learner who knows it has unlocked genuine cultural vocabulary.

variants.note: He put his foot in it — past tense.

meaning: To skip work; to play hooky; to take an unauthorized day off.
origin: Possibly from woad dyeing (Blaufärben), where cloth soaked in blue dye had to rest — workers could take the day off while the vat sat idle. Alternatively, from Blue Monday (Saint Monday), the tradition of taking Mondays off.

variants.note: Written as two words — both spellings are common.
He's taking the day off — present tense.

meaning: To give one's two cents; to add an unsolicited opinion.
origin: Mustard (Senf) was a pungent, widely used condiment that was added to food even when unwanted. 'Adding your mustard' to a conversation suggests imposing a strong-flavored opinion where it wasn't sought.

variants.note: To add one's mustard — slightly more formal verb.

variants.surface: muss er immer seinen Senf dazugeben
variants.note: He always has to put his two cents in.

meaning: To be blind to what's obvious; to not see what's right in front of one.
origin: Tomatoes (Tomaten) on the eyes — large round objects blocking the view. A comic image emphasizing willful or oblivious blindness. More colorful than the English 'scales on one's eyes.'
why_it_matters: Strikingly visual and playful. Unique to German among major European languages. Learning it builds awareness of German's penchant for vegetable-based idiom.

variants.surface: du hast wohl Tomaten auf den Augen
variants.note: You must have tomatoes on your eyes — direct address, exasperated.

meaning: To beat around the hot porridge; to avoid the subject.
origin: Hot porridge (heißer Brei) cannot be eaten immediately — one circles around it, blowing on it, waiting. The image of circling without committing is used for evasive speech. Compare French 'tourner autour du pot'.

variants.note: To walk around the hot porridge — motion variant.

variants.surface: rede nicht um den heißen Brei herum
variants.note: Don't beat around the bush — imperative.

meaning: Through thick and thin; in good times and bad.
origin: Thick (dick) and thin (dünn) referred to thick and thin forest terrain — alternating dense thicket and open paths. Through both kinds of terrain suggests loyalty regardless of conditions. Parallel to English 'through thick and thin.'

variants.note: To go through thick and thin — with verb.

meaning: The fifth wheel; an unnecessary person.
origin: A wagon needs four wheels — a fifth is purely superfluous. The expression captures the feeling of being unwanted or redundant in a group. Found in early modern German. The English 'fifth wheel' shares the same image.

variants.surface: das fünfte Rad am Wagen sein
variants.note: To be the fifth wheel — with sein (to be).

variants.surface: er ist das fünfte Rad am Wagen
variants.note: He's the odd one out.

meaning: To live behind the moon; to be out of touch with reality.
origin: The moon's far side (the dark side) was invisible — living there means existing beyond what the world can see or know. A person 'behind the moon' is ignorant of current events and modern life.

variants.surface: du lebst wohl hinter dem Mond
variants.note: You must be living on the moon — direct address.
Colloquial contraction with 'hinterm'.

meaning: Like a shot from a pistol; immediately; instantaneously.
origin: The speed of a bullet fired from a pistol: as fast as possible. Used to describe instant responses, especially verbal ones. 'Er antwortete wie aus der Pistole geschossen' — he answered without a moment's hesitation.

variants.surface: er antwortete wie aus der Pistole geschossen
variants.note: He answered like a shot.

meaning: To hang in the balance; to be on the verge; to be uncertain.
origin: Kippe can mean a tipping point, a see-saw, or the edge of a surface. Something balanced on the Kippe can fall either way — an apt metaphor for an undecided situation.

variants.note: It's hanging in the balance.
Variant using Kante (edge) — same meaning.

meaning: To buy a pig in a poke; to buy something without examining it first.
origin: Medieval market fraud: merchants sold a (presumably valuable) cat in a bag instead of the promised pig. Letting the cat out of the bag (den Braten riechen) revealed the trick. Compare English 'pig in a poke' and French 'acheter chat en poche.'

variants.note: Indefinite article variant — slightly more generic.

meaning: To step on the gas; to speed up; to increase effort.
origin: Zahn (tooth) as slang for speed — possibly from cycling, where adding a gear (Zahn = tooth on a cog) means pedaling faster. The image is mechanical: adding a cog increases velocity.

variants.note: Speed it up — imperative.
To step it up a notch further.

canonical: zwei Fliegen mit einer Klappe schlagen
meaning: To kill two birds with one stone.
origin: Klappe (flap, shutter) rather than stone: German uses a fly swatter or door flap hitting two flies. The same concept as English but with an indoor domestic image. Also attested in Dutch and other Germanic languages.

variants.surface: zwei Fliegen mit einer Klappe schlagen

variants.note: Elliptical — the verb often dropped in parenthetical use.

meaning: To tar everyone with the same brush; to treat everyone identically without distinction.
origin: Scheren (to shear, to comb) + Kamm (comb): running one comb over everyone flattens all differences. The shearing image evokes sheep — treating people as undifferentiated flock.

variants.note: Elliptical — verb dropped in informal use.

variants.surface: man kann nicht alle über einen Kamm scheren
variants.note: You can't treat everyone the same — negated form.

meaning: To fall through; to not happen; to come to nothing.
origin: Something that falls into water disappears or is ruined — a plan 'fallen into water' has dissolved. The aquatic metaphor for failure is common across Germanic languages.

variants.surface: der Plan ist ins Wasser gefallen
variants.note: The plan fell through — past tense.
Fallen through — participial use.

meaning: To get the cow off the ice; to solve a tricky problem.
origin: A cow on ice is in a precarious, almost comic predicament — heavy, unstable, unable to walk safely. Getting it off requires ingenuity and care. The image encodes both the difficulty and the urgency of problem-solving.
why_it_matters: Unique to German. Learners of German often encounter it and find it initially baffling — a perfect example of opaque cultural metaphor.

variants.surface: wir müssen die Kuh vom Eis bringen
variants.note: We need to get the cow off the ice — urgent form.
The cow is off the ice — the problem is solved.

meaning: To use a sledgehammer to crack a nut; to use disproportionate means.
origin: Firing a cannon (Kanone) at sparrows (Spatzen) is absurdly excessive. The expression critiques the mismatch between powerful means and trivial ends. Common in business, politics, and everyday critique.

variants.note: Elliptical parenthetical.

variants.surface: das ist mit Kanonen auf Spatzen geschossen
variants.note: That's using a sledgehammer to crack a nut.

meaning: To live large; to live extravagantly; to live beyond one's means.
origin: In medieval times, long pointed shoe tips (Schnabelschuhe) indicated social rank — longer tips = higher status. Living on a 'large foot' referenced the conspicuous luxury of aristocratic footwear.
why_it_matters: Contrast with 'auf gutem Fuß stehen' (to be on good terms with someone) — same Fuß, different meaning. Distinguishing these builds lexical precision.

variants.note: With dative -e ending — older, still seen in formal writing.
He's living large.

meaning: To keep one's fingers crossed for someone; to wish someone luck.
origin: In Roman gladiatorial combat, pressing the thumb down (or closing the fist around it) was a gesture of mercy or good will. The Germanic tradition adopted the thumb-pressing gesture as a luck charm. Compare English 'fingers crossed.'

variants.note: I'm crossing my fingers for you.
Elliptical imperative or standalone expression.

meaning: To call a spade a spade; to name something clearly and directly.
origin: Naming the child (das Kind) by its name — a metaphor for honest, direct identification of something others might hesitate to name. Attested in early modern German and parallel to Erasmian humanist rhetoric about plain speech.

variants.surface: das Kind beim richtigen Namen nennen
variants.note: Call the child by its right name — with emphasis on 'right'.

meaning: To be on the go; to be about to leave; to be in a hurry.
origin: Sprung (jump, leap) — being on the verge of jumping suggests imminent motion. The expression captures a moment of readiness before departure, widely used in casual German.

variants.note: I'm just on my way out.
He's about to leave.

meaning: Not to lift a finger; to make no effort whatsoever.
origin: Making a finger crooked (krumm machen) is the smallest possible physical movement. Refusing even that gesture emphasizes total passivity. The negation 'keinen' + minimal action = maximal laziness.

variants.note: He won't lift a finger.

meaning: To give up; to throw in the towel; to abandon one's efforts.
origin: Flinte (musket, rifle) + Korn (grain field): a soldier who throws their weapon into a grain field has surrendered or deserted. The battlefield image evokes hopeless retreat. More vivid than English 'throw in the towel.'

variants.surface: er hat die Flinte ins Korn geworfen
variants.note: He's given up — past tense.

variants.surface: wirf nicht gleich die Flinte ins Korn
variants.note: Don't give up so quickly — imperative.

meaning: To have someone in one's sights; to be watching someone closely with suspicion.
origin: Kieker (telescope, binoculars) from maritime and military use. 'Having someone through the telescope' means tracking them closely. The expression implies sustained surveillance and mild hostility.

variants.surface: er hat mich auf dem Kieker
variants.note: He's got me in his sights.

meaning: To get oneself into a mess; to land oneself in trouble.
origin: Nesseln (stinging nettles): sitting down in a nettle patch causes immediate, painful consequences. A visceral image for the result of reckless action. Common in Austrian and South German usage.

variants.surface: er hat sich in die Nesseln gesetzt
variants.note: He got himself into trouble.

meaning: To smell a rat; to sense that something is not right.
origin: Braten (roast meat): the smell of something cooking alerts you to what is happening before you see it. 'Smelling the roast' = detecting a situation before it is revealed. Related to the 'cat in the bag' cluster of medieval marketplace fraud idioms.

variants.note: I smelled a rat — past tense.

variants.surface: er hat den Braten schnell gerochen
variants.note: He caught on quickly.

meaning: To go to bat for someone; to stand up for someone; to make a case on their behalf.
origin: Medieval jousting: breaking a lance in tournament combat for someone's honor was an act of personal advocacy and loyalty. The expression retains the chivalric register of active, public defense of another.

variants.note: Go to bat for him — with pronoun.

variants.surface: er brach eine Lanze für sie
variants.note: He stood up for her — past tense.

meaning: Hook, line, and sinker; completely; lock, stock, and barrel.
origin: Skin (Haut) and hair (Haare) represent the whole person — external covering included. 'With skin and hair' = entirely, without leaving anything behind. Used of both consuming something completely and being completely ensnared.

variants.surface: er hat es mit Haut und Haaren geglaubt
variants.note: He swallowed it hook, line, and sinker.

variants.surface: sie ist mit Haut und Haaren verliebt
variants.note: She's head over heels in love.

meaning: To be over the worst; to be out of the woods; to have passed the crisis.
origin: The mountain (Berg) as an obstacle to be surmounted: once you're over the peak, the worst of the climb is behind you. Applied to illness, crisis, and difficulty. Compare English 'over the hill' (though German's meaning is more positive).

variants.note: He's over the worst.
Not out of the woods yet.

meaning: To be taken in; to fall for a trick; to be fooled.
origin: Leim (glue, birdlime): hunters coated branches with sticky birdlime to catch birds — they landed and stuck. 'Going onto the glue' = being trapped by a deception. The image is ancient and appears across European languages.

variants.surface: er ist auf den Leim gegangen
variants.note: He fell for it — past tense.
To be taken in by someone — with dative agent.

meaning: To be stranded; to be in a fix; to have run out of resources.
origin: Trockenen (dry land): a fish or boat stranded on dry land cannot move. The image captures helplessness from lack of the usual medium — a sailor without water, or a person without money or support.

variants.note: Contracted form without -en, also common.
He's high and dry.
